subheadline: Zerg works with teams whose products depend on hard technical systems: hardware, simulations, data and control planes, vendor integrations, migrations, and production workflows. We model the environment, build the software, validate against real signals, and keep it operating as the system changes.
value_proposition: Zerg AI is an autonomous code generation platform that continuously builds, validates against real signals, and maintains software for hard technical systems that normal engineering teams struggle to own.
target_audience: Engineering and platform teams at companies running mission-critical systems involving hardware, vendor APIs, data pipelines, large-scale migrations, CVE remediation, or operational workflows with high correctness requirements.

metrics: 24 days for a large JavaScript-to-TypeScript migration

pricing.price_anchoring: Frames value as enabling work that was previously 'uneconomic' — shipping integrations at a cadence that used to require a much larger team.

features: Map: Models real systems from codebases, APIs, documents, operators, hardware, tests, and production rules
Build: Generates integrations, internal tools, migration code, test harnesses, and operational applications
Prove: Validates against live services, simulators, devices, and production signals beyond unit tests
Run: Monitors for drift in requirements, APIs, or dependencies and creates follow-on fixes autonomously
Data Connectors: First-class integrations with email, files, calendars, CRMs, warehouses, vendor APIs, cloud infrastructure, and custom hardware
differentiators: 'We do not stop at prompt-to-code' — explicit rejection of one-shot code generation
Hardware-in-the-loop validation for systems where correctness depends on real signals
Continuous operating loop that keeps watching and fixing after initial delivery
Targets problems 'where the problem has real interfaces, live state, and expensive correctness requirements'
trust_signals: Specific metric: 24-day production JS-to-TS migration with reviewable checkpoints
Specific metric: 2-3 production integrations shipped per week for platform teams
Hardware-in-the-loop workflow claim with signal validation

design_patterns.layout: Long-form vertical scroll with clearly delineated named sections (Mission, Runtime, Proof, Use Cases); numbered list patterns for frameworks and use cases; a four-step operating loop visualization
design_patterns.color_scheme: Cannot confirm from text alone; tone suggests dark/technical palette typical of infrastructure tooling
design_patterns.typography: Technical, structured; heavy use of numbered lists and section labels
notable_patterns: 'Live' indicator badge on operating loop suggesting real-time visualization

emotional_tone: Confident and technical with a mission-driven undertone; avoids hype in favor of precision and operational seriousness
key_objections_addressed: 'The hard part is not generating code once' — addresses skepticism that AI just generates throwaway code
Validation against real signals, not just unit tests — addresses concern about AI code quality in production
Post-delivery monitoring and drift detection — addresses concern about maintenance and long-term reliability
Safe access with permissions, memory, and verification — addresses concern about AI agents having unsafe system access
Reviewable checkpoints mentioned in migration proof — addresses concern about loss of human oversight
missing_or_weak: Zero named customers or company logos — no social proof from recognizable organizations
No testimonials or quotes from actual users
No team page or founder credibility signals visible
Waitlist/contact-sales model with no self-serve option creates friction for evaluation
No case study depth — metrics are stated but not attributed to specific companies
No pricing transparency even at a tier level
No security certifications, SOC 2, or compliance badges despite targeting enterprise/mission-critical buyers
Product screenshots, demos, or video walkthroughs appear absent
ZDE and ZTC products mentioned but not explained beyond acronym expansions
standout_elements: Extremely sharp ICP targeting — the page actively narrows its audience to teams with 'hard technical systems', which builds credibility with that audience
The four-stage operating loop (Map, Build, Prove, Run) is a clear and memorable framework that differentiates from generic AI coding tools
Explicit rejection of 'prompt-to-code' positions the product above commoditized AI tools
Hardware-in-the-loop and signal validation claims are rare and compelling for the target audience
'Bring us the system no one wants to own' is a memorable and emotionally resonant closing CTA framing
Use case specificity (CVE remediation, ETL pipelines, code migration) avoids vagueness common in AI landing pages
